narrative_blocks.header: Named for the uncle lost over France
narrative_blocks.body: Quentin Roosevelt, the president's youngest son, was shot down over France in 1918. Five years later this niece was born and given his name to keep — Judith Quentin Derby.

narrative_blocks.header: She married into a dynasty of scientists
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband and his father both made their names studying the brain and the eye, a rare scientific lineage. She died in 1973, in her forties, the mother of three.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law, who built the Ames room illusion
was her mother-in-law, wife of the Ames-room scientist

bio.bio_blurb: TR's granddaughter; wife of neuroscientist Del Ames

narrative_blocks.header: Named for the uncle lost over France
narrative_blocks.body: Quentin Roosevelt, the president's youngest son, was shot down over France in 1918. Five years later this niece was born and given his name to keep — Judith Quentin Derby.

narrative_blocks.header: She married into a dynasty of scientists
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband and his father both made their names studying the brain and the eye, a rare scientific lineage. She died in 1973, in her forties, the mother of three.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law, who built the Ames room illusion
was her mother-in-law, wife of the Ames-room scientist

bio.bio_blurb: TR's granddaughter; wife of neuroscientist Del Ames

notable.notable_blurb: Harvard neuroscientist; created the Ames medium

narrative_blocks.header: The fluid that keeps brain tissue alive
narrative_blocks.body: He devised an artificial cerebrospinal fluid, the Ames medium, that lets scientists keep nervous-system tissue working for days outside the body. It remains the standard solution for the purpose today.

narrative_blocks.header: The no-reflow phenomenon
narrative_blocks.body: Working with rabbit brains, he showed that after enough minutes without blood the smallest vessels clamp shut and will not refill, the first irreversible injury of a stroke. The finding later proved true in the heart and other organs.

narrative_blocks.header: Skiing competitively into his late eighties
narrative_blocks.body: Class valedictorian at Exeter and Phi Beta Kappa at Harvard, he kept racing in masters ski events until almost ninety. He was last seen on cross-country skis at ninety-six.

cross_connections.display_label: pioneered cardiac defibrillation as a Hooker physiologist

bio.bio_blurb: Harvard neuroscientist; created the Ames medium

narrative_blocks.header: Division surgeon under fire in France
narrative_blocks.body: He served fourteen months as surgeon of the Second Infantry Division, wounded in the field and decorated by France with the Croix de Guerre and the Legion of Honor. The American army added its Distinguished Service Medal.

narrative_blocks.header: He rebuilt hospital care across Long Island
narrative_blocks.body: For some thirty years after the war he led surgery at the Glen Cove hospital and drove the founding of a new laboratory there, later named in his honor. Friends raised a quarter of a million dollars to build it.

narrative_blocks.header: A hunter in his father-in-law's mold
narrative_blocks.body: Like Theodore Roosevelt he loved big game, stalking Kodiak bear in Alaska and filming moose on long northern trips. He titled his own war memoir Wade in, Sanitary.

bio.bio_blurb: Surgeon; TR's son-in-law and WWI division surgeon

military_service.notes: Division surgeon in France; Croix de Guerre, Legion of Honor, Distinguished Service Medal.

narrative_blocks.header: She nursed the wounded in wartime France
narrative_blocks.body: Early in the First World War she went to Paris with her husband and worked at the American Ambulance Hospital while he operated on men brought in from the front. She came home to decades of civic and hospital work on Long Island.

narrative_blocks.header: She made Sagamore Hill a national shrine
narrative_blocks.body: More than anyone she pushed to preserve her father's home, Sagamore Hill, as a public historic site, and guarded the Roosevelt papers given to Harvard. In Oyster Bay, where she fought for low-income housing, neighbors called her the Queen of Oyster Bay.

narrative_blocks.header: One of the museum's first women trustees
narrative_blocks.body: She became one of the first two women on the board of the American Museum of Natural History, the institution her grandfather had helped found. She also chaired the Nassau County Nursing Service and led the county's Red Cross.

narrative_blocks.header: Painted in her Red Cross uniform
narrative_blocks.body: For her formal family portrait she chose to sit in her Red Cross uniform rather than evening dress and jewels. She gave the organization some sixty years and chaired its Nassau County work.

narrative_blocks.header: The Roosevelt child who stayed close to home
narrative_blocks.body: Of all the president's children she was the steady one, anchoring the family at Oyster Bay long after her brothers scattered to wars and far places. She married the surgeon who would tend the wounded beside her in France.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law, a New York eye-surgery pioneer
was her mother-in-law, painted by John Singer Sargent

bio.bio_blurb: TR's only surviving daughter by Edith Carow

narrative_blocks.header: A pioneer of New York eye surgery
narrative_blocks.body: Trained in Berlin under the great von Graefe, he became a leading ophthalmic surgeon in New York and an officer of the national eye-surgeons' society. He held posts at the New York Eye and Ear Infirmary and other hospitals.

narrative_blocks.header: He fought contagious blindness in the schools
narrative_blocks.body: As a public-health investigator he traced contagious eye disease through New York's tenements and institutions, then pushed a law through the legislature to protect children in residential schools. Hospitals for such cases followed his reports.

cross_connections.display_label: married his son Richard, the wartime division surgeon

narrative_blocks.header: Sargent painted her in a full-length portrait
narrative_blocks.body: John Singer Sargent painted her in 1888, a tall canvas shown that spring with the Society of American Artists. The portrait stayed in the family for generations.

cross_connections.display_label: married her son Richard, the wartime division surgeon

notable.notable_blurb: 26th US President; Rough Rider; Nobel Peace laureate

cross_connections.display_label: his Harvard ornithology partner on an 1877 bird list
Trust-busted Morgan's Northern Securities Company in 1904
designed the townhouse his family used near the White House
drew up the guardianship papers for his children
the Columbia president and Nobel laureate he befriended

military_service.notes: Led the assault on the San Juan Heights, 1898; Medal of Honor awarded in 2001.

narrative_blocks.header: He married the childhood friend next door
narrative_blocks.body: Roosevelt married Edith Kermit Carow in London in 1886, his second wife and a granddaughter of the Union general Daniel Tyler. Through her he entered the Tyler family, with its Edwards and Hooker roots in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard ornithology friendship became his first publication
narrative_blocks.body: He met Henry Davis Minot, a Boston-based naturalist, as a Harvard freshman in 1877. They spent vacations listing birds in the Adirondacks and co-authored 'The Summer Birds of the Adirondacks' that fall — his first published work.

narrative_blocks.header: Trust-busted J.P. Morgan's Northern Securities Company
narrative_blocks.body: His administration brought the antitrust suit that dissolved Morgan's Northern Securities holding company in 1904, ending the merger of the Northern Pacific and Great Northern railroads. Morgan reportedly told him 'Send your man to my man and they can fix it up' — the President refused, and the Supreme Court sided with the government.

narrative_blocks.header: He befriended Columbia's president Nicholas Butler
narrative_blocks.body: Roosevelt's circle included Nicholas Murray Butler, the long-serving Columbia president and 1931 Nobel peace laureate, whom he nicknamed Nicholas Miraculous. The two Republican allies stayed close from Roosevelt's rise through the White House years.

narrative_blocks.header: The Roosevelts borrowed a famous architect's townhouse
narrative_blocks.body: When the White House was torn up for renovation in 1902, the family decamped to a townhouse belonging to the firm of Carrère and Hastings. Its partner Thomas Hastings would go on to design the New York Public Library and Arlington's Tomb of the Unknowns.

notable.notable_blurb: First Lady; wife of Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: She built the modern First Lady's office
narrative_blocks.body: Edith hired the first salaried social secretary for a First Lady and directed the 1902 White House renovation that created the West Wing. Historians credit her with shaping the modern institution of the role.

narrative_blocks.header: She burned a lifetime of love letters
narrative_blocks.body: After Theodore died, the intensely private Edith destroyed the letters he had written her across their courtship and marriage. It is part of why her inner life stayed, by her own design, almost unknowable.

narrative_blocks.header: She wrote her own Connecticut ancestors' history
narrative_blocks.body: Late in life Edith researched her mother's Tyler family and co-wrote a history of their colonial Connecticut roots. She bought the family's ancestral home in Brooklyn, Connecticut, returning there each summer.

narrative_blocks.header: She stumped for Hoover to disown Franklin
narrative_blocks.body: Edith kept out of politics for decades, then made a rare appearance for Herbert Hoover in 1932. Part of her motive was to remind voters that the Democratic nominee, Franklin Roosevelt, was only a distant cousin and not her son.

narrative_blocks.header: Theodore regretted overruling her judgment
narrative_blocks.body: A childhood neighbor whom Theodore Roosevelt married in 1886, Edith ran Sagamore Hill and raised six children while quietly advising him on people and politics. Roosevelt admitted he was worse off whenever he ignored her counsel.

cross_connections.display_label: was her stepdaughter, the capital’s sharpest wit
her husband President Roosevelt's Hartford-born mother

bio.bio_blurb: First Lady; wife of Theodore Roosevelt

career.role: First Lady of the United States
